EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak low pressure tracked up the Mid-Atlantic Coast. A cold air mass in place caused precipitation with this low to fall in the form of snow and ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A glaze of ice was estimated based on observations nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked up the Appalachians. Warm and moist air overran the colder air in place, resulting in precipitation. There was a cold layer near the surface that caused freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A glaze of ice was estimated based on observations nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually moist air mass and light winds led to areas of dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility was reported to be around one-quarter mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wave of low pressure passed by to the south on the 20th. At the same time, high pressure was building to the north and this caused cold air to filter in from New England. Rain changed to a wintry mix on the 20th and a light wintry mix continued through the night of the 20th. Coastal low pressure developed along the Mid-Atlantic Coast on the morning of the 21st. This caused a period of heavier precipitation to fall mainly in the form of snow. The snow tapered off later in the day as the low moved off to the north and east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix occurred on the 20th before a round of heavier snowfall developed during the morning of the 21st. Snowfall totaled up to 4.1 inches at the National Zoo and 2.0 inches at Anacostia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving eastward from the central United States on Thursday March 1st, intensified rapidly as it moved offshore Thursday night and early Friday, deepening to 974 mb by the evening of Friday March 2nd.||Strong winds were present in the low levels of the atmosphere due to the strengthening pressure gradient, and were able to mix down to the surface in strong wind gusts under northwest flow cold air advection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down blocking the 4500 Block of Fessenden Street. A 64 mph was reported near Silver Spring. A wind gust of 66 mph was reported at Catholic University.||A tree fell into a building along the 100 Block of Joliet Street Southwest, resulting in an injury. Emergency Management reported around 172 trees down due to high wind across the District of Columbia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong onshore flow led to moderate tidal flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The water level at Maine Avenue indicated that the unprotected area on the Southwest Waterfront at the DC seafood market began to flood. Water approached parts of the Hains Point Loop Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong onshore flow led to moderate tidal flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The water level at Maine Avenue indicated that the unprotected area on the Southwest Waterfront at the DC seafood market began to flood. Water approached parts of the Hains Point Loop Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure and its associated cold front passed through during the early morning hours of April 16th. Heavy rain of 1.5 to 2 inches fell in the District, causing flash flooding followed by flooding of Rock Creek which continued into early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water levels on Rock Creek rapidly rose above the 7 foot flood stage, causing flooding within Rock Creek Park in the District.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure and its associated cold front passed through during the early morning hours of April 16th. Heavy rain of 1.5 to 2 inches fell in the District, causing flash flooding followed by flooding of Rock Creek which continued into early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor street flooding was reported at the intersection of Canal Road and Arizona Avenue in Northwest DC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure and its associated cold front passed through during the early morning hours of April 16th. Heavy rain of 1.5 to 2 inches fell in the District, causing flash flooding followed by flooding of Rock Creek which continued into early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain created standing water, making a portion of Maine Avenue near I-395 impassible.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed by to the west. Warm and moist air overran the arctic air in place causing a period of freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light glaze of ice was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple waves of low pressure brought some precipitation to the area, and there was enough cold air ahead of this system for the precipitation type to be snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 0.7 inches at the National Arboretum.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure rapidly intensified offshore. A period of snow developed due to the lows rapid intensification. Although snow amounts were light, it impacted the morning rush thanks to cold temperatures that allowed the light snow to stick.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 0.6 inches at the National Zoo and 0.5 inches at the National Arboretum.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level high along with surface high over the western Atlantic led to hot and humid conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices were around 105 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level high along with surface high over the western Atlantic led to hot and humid conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices were around 110 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An influx of freshwater due to heavy rain led to increased water level anomalies and coastal flooding along the Potomac River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The gauge at Southwest Waterfront (WASD2) indicated moderate flooding. At this stage, the unprotected area on the southwest waterfront at the DC seafood market begins to flood. Water also approaches parts of the Hains Point Loop Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An influx of freshwater due to heavy rain led to increased water level anomalies and coastal flooding along the Potomac River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The gauge at Wisconsin Avenue (GTND2) indicated moderate flooding. At this stage, Washington Harbour begins to flood if floodgates are not in place. More than a third of Roosevelt Island is also covered by water and backwater flooding of Rock Creek in Georgetown begins.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increased southerly and onshore flow in addition to an influx of freshwater due to heavy rain led to increased water level anomalies and coastal flooding along the Potomac River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The gauge at Southwest Waterfront (WASD2) indicated moderate flooding. At this stage, the unprotected area on the southwest waterfront at the DC seafood market begins to flood. Water also approaches parts of the Hains Point Loop Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies, falling temperatures, and abundant low level moisture led to the formation of dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observations from around the area indicate visibility fell to a quarter of a mile or less in dense fog.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure and its associated cold front passed through during the early morning hours of April 16th. Heavy rain of 1.5 to 2 inches fell in the District, causing flash flooding followed by flooding of Rock Creek which continued into early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS stream gauge at Rock Creek remained above 7 feet for several hours, although it was no longer rapidly rising. The peak level of 7.92 feet occurred at 1045 AM EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southerly flow pumped in plenty of moisture from the Atlantic while a slow moving cold front approached from the west. Copious amounts of moisture ahead of the boundary led to showers and thunderstorms. Torrential downpours combined with convection training over the same areas led to some flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 4000 block of 1st Street was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low near the region combined with high instability and increasing winds aloft to produce thunderstorms, some of which became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree was blown down in the 100 block of Seaton Place.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak boundary triggered some showers and thunderstorms. Locally heavy rain caused an isolated flooding incident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broad Branch Creek rose out of its banks causing flooding at 27th Street and Broad Branch Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low near the region combined with high instability and increasing winds aloft to produce thunderstorms, some of which became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree was blown down near the intersection of 41st Street and Emery Place.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low near the region combined with high instability and increasing winds aloft to produce thunderstorms, some of which became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree was blown down into a house in the 4900 block of 30th Street NW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed by to the west toward the Great Lakes. The warm front associated with the low moved through the area while the cold front associated with the low approached from the west. There was enough lift associated with the warm front to combine with a warm and humid atmosphere, resulting in showers and thunderstorms. Rainfall was heavy at times which resulted in flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MLK Jr. Avenue was closed due to high water at the intersection with Morris Road Southeast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed by to the west toward the Great Lakes. The warm front associated with the low moved through the area while the cold front associated with the low approached from the west. There was enough lift associated with the warm front to combine with a warm and humid atmosphere, resulting in showers and thunderstorms. Rainfall was heavy at times which resulted in flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Piney Branch Parkway was closed due to high water near the intersection with Arkansas Avenue Northwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper-level low and associated area of surface low pressure approached the region from southwest on the 15th. Strong forcing for ascent and ample moisture transport in advance of the upper level low led to a prolonged period of rain across the region. Eventually low pressure transferred to the coast before precipitation wound down on the 16th. Widespread rainfall totals of 2-4 inches were observed across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gage on Rock Creek at Sherrill Drive/DC exceeded the 7 foot flood stage during the indicated period. Water reached several portions of the Valley Trail between Picnic Areas 7 and 10 in Rock Creek Park, and completely covered the Rolling Meadows Trailbridge. The peak level of 8.02 feet occurred at 3:45 AM on the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ridging at the surface and aloft led to hot and humid conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices were around 105 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low pressure moved up the Atlantic seaboard. Copious moisture spread northward across the District of Columbia. Rainfall totals of 3 to 5 inches were observed, causing flooding, especially along Rock Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding on Rock Creek Parkway between I-66 and the Kennedy Center forced the closure of the northbound lanes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rain fell in Washington DC on the afternoon of July 25th. This caused flash flooding at rush hour in the metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High rushing water reported over Broad Branch Road in Northwest DC with Broad Branch out of its banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rain fell in Washington DC on the afternoon of July 25th. This caused flash flooding at rush hour in the metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mail truck overturned in swift water on Broad Branch Road in Northwest DC near the Davenport Street/Grant Road intersection. The occupant was rescued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rain fell in Washington DC on the afternoon of July 25th. This caused flash flooding at rush hour in the metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Canal Road NW flooded and closed between Arizona Avenue and MacArthur Boulevard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low pressure moved up the Atlantic seaboard. Copious moisture spread northward across the District of Columbia. Rainfall totals of 3 to 5 inches were observed, causing flooding, especially along Rock Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rock Creek Parkway flooded and closed at Massachusetts Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low pressure moved up the Atlantic seaboard. Copious moisture spread northward across the District of Columbia. Rainfall totals of 3 to 5 inches were observed, causing flooding, especially along Rock Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gage on Rock Creek in Washington DC exceeded the flood stage of 7 feet during the indicated time period. The peak level of 8.40 feet was observed at 3:45am EST on July 22nd. Valley Trail and other portions of Rock Creek Park flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak trough caused thunderstorms to develop in a hot and humid air mass. The heat and humidity led to a couple storms becoming severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was down, blocking the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak trough caused thunderstorms to develop in a hot and humid air mass. The heat and humidity led to a couple storms becoming severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was down, blocking the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed through the area on the 14th. The low tracked along a boundary that separated warm and humid air to the south and west from cooler marine air to the north and east. The warm and humid air led to an unstable atmosphere, and there was enough instability for storms to become severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down along 12th street and Maryland Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freshwater from heavy rainfall upstream piled up along the upper portions of the Tidal Potomac River, resulting in moderate flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to gauge levels the river crested near 7.5 feet at Wisconsin Avenue (GTND2). At 7.5 feet, Washington Harbour will begin to flood if floodgates are not in place. More than a third of Roosevelt Island is covered by water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freshwater upstream in the Potomac rushed downstream, and this resulted in moderate flooding along the Tidal Basin in Washington DC.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The gauge at the Southwest Waterfront (GTND2) indicated moderate flooding. Backwater flooding of Rock Creek in Georgetown took place. Washington Harbour would begin to flood if floodgates were not in place. More than a third of Roosevelt Island was covered by water. Water covered the boardwalk at Washington Harbour. Downstream, water was out of the banks in a few low spots between Fletcher's Boathouse and Interstate 66.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rain fell in Washington DC on the afternoon of July 25th. This caused flash flooding at rush hour in the metro area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain around 3 inches fell just north of Washington DC on June 3rd, causing flooding in the Rock Creek area of the District.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gage on Rock Creek in Rock Creek Park exceeded the 7 foot flood stage during the indicated period. The peak level of 8.57 feet occurred at 4:45pm on June 3rd. Portions of Rock Creek Park were flooded within Washington DC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure, clear skies, and light winds led to temperatures that fell below freezing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observations from around the area indicate that temperatures fell below 32 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon showers and thunderstorms produced around an inch of rain, producing some urban flooding in the DC metropolitan area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding on Constitution Avenue in Northwest DC near the Washington Monument.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed across the southeastern United States on the night of November 14th, and tracked northeastward along the North and South Carolina coastlines during the daytime hours on the 15th, strengthening to around 995mb on the night of the 15th as it continued moving northward through the Delmarva to New Jersey by the morning of the 16th. Further strengthening to around 985 mb occurred on the 16th as the system sped up and moved towards Nova Scotia. Widespread precipitation was brought to the region, including snow, sleet, and rain across the District.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches occurred, with the highest amount of 2 measured near the National Zoo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southerly onshore flow ahead of a strong low pressure system led to increased tidal anomalies and moderate coastal flooding across portions of the upper tidal Potomac.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The DC/Southwest Waterfront gauge (WASD2) indicated that moderate coastal flooding occurred. At this level, the unprotected area on the Southwest Waterfront at the DC seafood market begins to flood and water approaches parts of the Hains Point Loop Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seventh Street was closed due to high water approaching I-695 East.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The water level surpassed the flood stage of 7 feet, and crested at 7.68 feet at 4:15 AM on September 18th. Water reaches several portions of the Valley Trail between Picnic Areas 7 and 10 in Rock Creek Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary remain stalled over the region and tropical moisture was in place. This caused locally heavy rainfall. The steering flow aloft was weak so showers and storms moved slowly, which led to instances of flooding and flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After Hurricane Michael made landfall near Mexico Beach, Florida with maximum sustained winds of 155 mph and minimum central pressure of 919 mb on Wednesday October 10th, it weakened to a tropical storm as it tracked northeastward through Georgia and South Carolina. The tropical storm then tracked across North Carolina and southeast Virginia Thursday and Thursday night, October 11th, emerging back into the Atlantic Ocean early Friday morning, October 12th. As the system passed south of the region, heavy rain then caused instances of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down, including along River Road, Canal Road, and New Hampshire Avenue. A 43 knot (49 mph) gust was measured near Catholic University.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted trough approached the area on the 24th as surface low pressure tracked up toward the Great Lakes and secondary low pressure formed along the Carolina Coast. Lift ahead of the negatively tilted trough/within the left exit region of an upper-level jet streak led to a period of moderate to heavy rain through much of the day on the 24th. Rainfall totals were generally between 1-2 inches across the area, resulting in many instances of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gage on the Rock Creek in DC exceeded the 7 foot flood stage during the indicated period. Water reached several portions of the Valley Trail between Picnic Areas 7 and 10 in Rock Creek Park. The peak level of 7.37 feet occurred at 11:00 PM on the 24th.
